title: Quiz: Quantum Value Function Approximation
description: Test your understanding of quantum circuits for value function estimation

questions.question: How is Q(s,a) typically estimated from a quantum circuit?

options.text: By counting the number of gates
By measuring the expectation value of an observable like ⟨Z⟩

questions.explanation: Q-values are estimated by encoding state and action into a circuit, applying variational layers, and measuring the expectation value of an observable (e.g., ⟨Z₀⟩) as the Q-value.

questions.question: What is an advantage of using separate Q-networks per action?

options.text: Each action has dedicated expressive capacity

questions.explanation: Separate networks give each action its own variational parameters, potentially allowing more expressive value estimation per action, though at the cost of more parameters.

questions.question: In quantum TD learning, what is computed using the parameter-shift rule?

options.text: The gradient of Q(s,a) with respect to circuit parameters

questions.explanation: Parameter-shift computes ∂Q(s,a)/∂θ, which is then multiplied by the TD error to update parameters: θ += α * δ * ∇Q.

questions.question: Why is experience replay important for quantum Q-learning?

options.text: It reduces the number of qubits needed
It breaks correlation between consecutive samples and stabilizes training
It eliminates the need for gradients

questions.explanation: Experience replay stores transitions and samples randomly, breaking temporal correlations and providing more stable, i.i.d.-like training data.

questions.question: In quantum DQN, the target network is updated every step to match the main Q-network.
questions.explanation: False. The target network is updated slowly (periodically or via Polyak averaging) to provide stable TD targets, reducing oscillation during training.

questions.question: What is the difference between V(s) and Q(s,a)?

options.text: V uses more qubits than Q
V(s) is the value of a state; Q(s,a) is the value of taking action a in state s
V requires classical processing; Q is fully quantum

questions.explanation: V(s) estimates expected return from state s under a policy. Q(s,a) estimates expected return from taking action a in state s, then following the policy.

questions.question: What is semi-gradient TD learning?

options.text: Only differentiating through the current value, not the bootstrap target
Using gradients from every other step

questions.explanation: Semi-gradient TD treats the TD target (r + γQ(s',a')) as fixed when computing gradients, only differentiating through Q(s,a) to avoid instability.

questions.question: Which techniques help stabilize quantum Q-learning? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: All listed techniques help: (a) replay breaks correlations, (b) target networks provide stable targets, (c) clipping prevents large updates, (d) normalization keeps values in reasonable ranges.
